---
title: "Children at Risk: A Global UNICEF Analysis"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Table of Contents"
    number-sections: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Introduction

**Every child deserves a healthy and safe start in life.** Yet for millions of children across
the world, the reality is very different. The country they are born in, the wealth of their
nation, and access to basic healthcare all profoundly shape their chances of survival and
development.

This report uses data from **UNICEF** to explore global patterns in child welfare. Through
maps, rankings, economic comparisons, and trend analysis, we aim to answer key questions:

- Which countries and regions face the greatest challenges?
- Does a country's economic wealth translate into better outcomes for children?
- Are things improving over time, or are some countries being left behind?

> *"Children are not just the future — they are the present. What we do for them today
> defines what the world will look like tomorrow."*

---

## Data & Setup

The analysis uses two UNICEF datasets:

- **unicef_indicator_2.csv** — Country-level indicator values over time
- **unicef_metadata__1_.csv** — Country metadata including GDP, population, and life expectancy

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: true

import polars as pl
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import geopandas as gpd
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
from plotnine import *

# ── Load data ──────────────────────────────────────────────
indicator = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_2.csv")
metadata  = pl.read_csv("unicef_metadata__1_.csv")

print(f"Indicator dataset : {indicator.shape[0]:,} rows × {indicator.shape[1]} columns")
print(f"Metadata dataset  : {metadata.shape[0]:,} rows × {metadata.shape[1]} columns")
print(f"\nIndicator columns : {indicator.columns}")
print(f"\nMetadata columns  : {metadata.columns}")
```

---

## Data Transformation

```{python}
#| label: transform
#| echo: true

# ── Clean indicator ────────────────────────────────────────
indicator_clean = (
    indicator
    .rename({"alpha_3_code": "country_code"})
    .filter(pl.col("obs_value").is_not_null())
    .with_columns(pl.col("obs_value").cast(pl.Float64))
)

# ── Clean metadata — rename long column names ──────────────
metadata_clean = (
    metadata
    .rename({
        "Population, total":                       "population_total",
        "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)":      "gdp_per_capita",
        "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)": "life_expectancy_at_birth",
        "GNI (current US$)":                       "gni"
    })
    .select([
        "country_code",
        "country",
        "population_total",
        "gdp_per_capita",
        "life_expectancy_at_birth",
        "gni"
    ])
)

# ── Merge ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
merged = indicator_clean.join(metadata_clean, on="country_code", how="left")

latest_year = int(indicator_clean["time_period"].max())

print(f"Merged shape      : {merged.shape}")
print(f"Countries covered : {indicator_clean['country'].n_unique()}")
print(f"Latest year       : {latest_year}")
print(f"Year range        : {int(indicator_clean['time_period'].min())} – {latest_year}")
```

```{python}
#| label: latest-data
#| echo: true

# ── Aggregate to latest year per country ───────────────────
latest_data = (
    merged
    .filter(pl.col("time_period") == latest_year)
    .group_by(["country_code", "country", "gdp_per_capita", "life_expectancy_at_birth"])
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("avg_value"))
    .sort("avg_value", descending=True)
)

print(f"Countries in latest year ({latest_year}): {latest_data.shape[0]}")
print(latest_data.head(10))
```

---

## Global Overview {#global}

### Where Are Children Most Affected?

The world map below plots each country as a point, coloured by the indicator value.
**Darker red indicates higher values.** Even at a glance, a clear geographic pattern
emerges — countries in **sub-Saharan Africa** and **South Asia** consistently show the
highest figures, while wealthier nations in Europe and North America show much lower values.

This spatial inequality is not accidental. It reflects decades of underinvestment in
healthcare, education, and infrastructure in the developing world.

```{python}
#| label: fig-map
#| fig-cap: "World map showing UNICEF indicator values by country (latest available year)"
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 6

# Load world country geometry
world = gpd.read_file(
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/datasets/geo-countries/master/data/countries.geojson"
)

latest_pd = latest_data.to_pandas()

world_merged = world.merge(
    latest_pd,
    left_on="ISO_A3",
    right_on="country_code",
    how="left"
)

world_merged["lon"] = world_merged.geometry.centroid.x
world_merged["lat"] = world_merged.geometry.centroid.y
map_df = world_merged[["ISO_A3", "avg_value", "lon", "lat"]].dropna()

map_plot = (
    ggplot(map_df, aes(x="lon", y="lat", color="avg_value"))
    + geom_point(size=2.5, alpha=0.85)
    + scale_color_gradient(
        low="#fee8c8", high="#b30000",
        na_value="lightgrey",
        name="Indicator\nValue"
    )
    + labs(
        title=f"Global UNICEF Indicator — {latest_year}",
        subtitle="Each point represents a country | Grey = no data available",
        x="", y=""
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(12, 5),
        axis_text=element_blank(),
        axis_ticks=element_blank(),
        panel_grid=element_blank(),
        plot_title=element_text(size=14, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="grey")
    )
)
print(map_plot)
```

---

## Country Rankings {#rankings}

### Top 20 Most Affected Countries

While the world map shows the broad global picture, the bar chart below focuses on the
**top 20 countries** with the highest indicator values. These nations deserve particular
attention from the international community.

The ranking reveals that the burden is heavily concentrated in a small number of countries.
Addressing the needs of these nations could have an outsized impact on global child welfare.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bar
#| fig-cap: "Top 20 countries ranked by UNICEF indicator value (latest available year)"
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 8

top20 = (
    latest_data
    .sort("avg_value", descending=True)
    .head(20)
    .to_pandas()
)

bar_plot = (
    ggplot(top20, aes(x="reorder(country, avg_value)", y="avg_value", fill="avg_value"))
    + geom_bar(stat="identity")
    + coord_flip()
    + scale_fill_gradient(low="#fdbb84", high="#b30000", name="Value")
    + labs(
        title=f"Top 20 Countries — UNICEF Indicator ({latest_year})",
        subtitle="Countries sorted by average indicator value",
        x="Country",
        y="Average Indicator Value"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(10, 8),
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="grey"),
        legend_position="none"
    )
)
print(bar_plot)
```

---

## The Role of Wealth {#wealth}

### Does GDP per Capita Matter?

A central question in development economics is whether national wealth translates into
better outcomes for children. The scatterplot below compares **GDP per capita** (on a
log scale, to handle the wide range of values) against the UNICEF indicator for each country.

The **dashed black regression line** reveals the overall trend. The negative slope
confirms that **wealthier countries tend to have lower indicator values** — but the
relationship is far from perfect. Several countries perform much worse than their
income level would suggest, pointing to governance and policy failures beyond mere poverty.

```{python}
#| label: fig-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Scatterplot of UNICEF indicator vs GDP per capita with linear regression line"
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6

scatter_data = (
    merged
    .filter(pl.col("time_period") == latest_year)
    .filter(pl.col("gdp_per_capita").is_not_null())
    .filter(pl.col("obs_value").is_not_null())
    .to_pandas()
)

scatter_plot = (
    ggplot(scatter_data, aes(x="gdp_per_capita", y="obs_value"))
    + geom_point(color="#d7301f", alpha=0.6, size=3)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color="black", linetype="dashed", se=True)
    + scale_x_log10()
    + labs(
        title="UNICEF Indicator vs GDP per Capita",
        subtitle=f"Year: {latest_year} | X-axis on log scale | Dashed line = linear regression",
        x="GDP per Capita (USD, log scale)",
        y="Indicator Value"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(10, 6),
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="grey")
    )
)
print(scatter_plot)
```

---

## Trends Over Time {#trends}

### Are Things Getting Better?

Snapshots in time only tell part of the story. The time-series chart below tracks how
the indicator has changed across the **six countries with the most complete historical data**.

Some countries show a clear downward trend — a sign that interventions are working.
Others remain stubbornly flat or even show increases, a reminder that **progress is
never guaranteed** and sustained effort is essential.

```{python}
#| label: fig-timeseries
#| fig-cap: "Time-series of UNICEF indicator for the six countries with the most complete data"
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 6

# Select 6 countries with most data points
top_countries = (
    indicator_clean
    .group_by("country")
    .agg(pl.len().alias("n_years"))
    .sort("n_years", descending=True)
    .head(6)["country"]
    .to_list()
)

time_data = (
    indicator_clean
    .filter(pl.col("country").is_in(top_countries))
    .to_pandas()
)

ts_plot = (
    ggplot(time_data, aes(x="time_period", y="obs_value",
                          color="country", group="country"))
    + geom_line(size=1.2)
    + geom_point(size=2.5)
    + labs(
        title="UNICEF Indicator Over Time — Selected Countries",
        subtitle="Six countries chosen based on data completeness",
        x="Year",
        y="Indicator Value",
        color="Country"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(11, 6),
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, face="bold"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="grey"),
        legend_position="right"
    )
)
print(ts_plot)
```

---

## Conclusion

This analysis of UNICEF data reveals several important and sobering findings:

1. **Geographic inequality is stark** — the burden falls overwhelmingly on low-income
   countries, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia.

2. **Wealth matters, but incompletely** — while richer nations generally perform better,
   there are notable outliers, suggesting that policy choices matter as much as income.

3. **Progress is uneven** — some countries have made meaningful improvements over time,
   while others have stagnated or worsened, demonstrating that improvement requires
   sustained, targeted effort.

4. **Concentration of need** — the top 20 most affected countries carry a
   disproportionate share of the global burden, suggesting that focused international
   support could make a significant difference.

**Data-driven analysis like this is essential** for ensuring that UNICEF and its partners
can allocate resources where they are most needed and track whether those investments
are delivering results for the world's most vulnerable children.

---

*Data source: UNICEF. Analysis conducted using Python (polars, plotnine, geopandas).
Report rendered with Quarto.*
